posts.title: agents that work vs agents that demo
posts.hook: Most AI agent demos would fail in production within 48 hours.
posts.body:
Most AI agent demos would fail in production within 48 hours.

I've been running Claude Code agents on cron jobs for weeks now. Nightly content generation, data enrichment, repo maintenance. Stuff that actually runs unattended.

Here's what the demo culture misses:

1. Agents fail silently. You need logging, health checks, and alerting before you need capabilities.
2. Context windows are finite. Your agent that "remembers everything" doesn't. You need persistence layers, handoff protocols, checkpoint files.
3. Retries matter more than reasoning. The agent that recovers from a flaky API call at 2am beats the one that writes prettier code in a demo.

The gap between "look what this agent can do" and "this agent ran 30 nights straight without waking me up" is enormous. It's plumbing. Launchd plists, error handling, graceful degradation.

Nobody posts their launchd configs on LinkedIn. But that's the actual work.
posts.cta: What's the longest you've had an AI agent running autonomously without intervention? Genuinely curious where people are at.

remixes.label: infrastructure-first
remixes.hook: I spent more time on logging than on the actual AI agent logic. That ratio is correct.
remixes.body:
I spent more time on logging than on the actual AI agent logic. That ratio is correct.

My nightly cron agents have been running for weeks. Content generation, data enrichment, repo maintenance. The boring stuff that nobody screenshots for Twitter.

The first version had zero observability. It ran for three days, then silently failed for eleven more. I didn't notice until the database stopped updating.

What fixed it wasn't a better prompt or a smarter model. It was a launchd plist with KeepAlive, structured JSON logs, and a health check that pings me when output stops.

The unglamorous truth about production AI agents:

You need retry logic before you need chain-of-thought. You need dead letter queues before you need tool use. You need alerting before you need autonomy.

Every agent framework markets the capabilities. Nobody markets the operational scaffolding that keeps those capabilities running at 3am when you're asleep.

The agents that work aren't the ones with the most impressive demos. They're the ones with the most boring infrastructure wrapped around them.
remixes.cta: What's the first thing that broke when you moved an AI agent from demo to production?

remixes.label: demo-graveyard
remixes.hook: The graveyard of AI agent projects isn't filled with bad ideas. It's filled with great demos that nobody could operate.
remixes.body:
The graveyard of AI agent projects isn't filled with bad ideas. It's filled with great demos that nobody could operate.

I've shipped agents that run unattended on a Mac Mini. Nightly content pipelines, lead enrichment, automated repo maintenance. They're not impressive to watch. They just work.

The pattern I see in every failed agent project:

Phase 1: Build something cool in a notebook. Everyone's excited.
Phase 2: Deploy it somewhere. It works for 48 hours.
Phase 3: It breaks silently. Nobody notices for a week.
Phase 4: Someone restarts it manually. This becomes the operating model.
Phase 5: The project gets shelved because it's "not reliable enough."

The gap between phase 1 and a real system isn't intelligence. It's plumbing. Error handling, state management, graceful degradation, context window budgeting, cost controls.

I run Qwen locally for high-frequency tasks and route expensive API calls only where they matter. Not because I'm cheap. Because an agent that costs $47/day doesn't survive budget review.

Operational maturity isn't a feature. It's the whole product.
remixes.cta: How many agent projects in your org made it past the demo phase? Be honest.

tones.advisor:
If you're evaluating AI agents for production use, start with the operational layer, not the capabilities.

I've been running Claude Code agents on cron jobs for weeks. Nightly content generation, data enrichment, repo maintenance. These run unattended, on schedule, without human intervention.

Here's what I recommend focusing on before you scale agent deployments:

First, build your observability stack. Agents fail silently. Without structured logging, health checks, and alerting, you won't know something broke until downstream systems start showing stale data.

Second, budget your context windows deliberately. They're finite resources. Treat them like memory allocation, not magic. Every token you waste on redundant context is latency and cost you're eating unnecessarily.

Third, route models by task economics. Run cheap local models for high-frequency classification. Reserve API calls for tasks that genuinely need frontier capability.

Fourth, design for graceful failure. Your agent will crash. Your API will rate-limit. Your context will overflow. The question is whether your system recovers automatically or pages someone at 3am.

The companies getting real value from agents right now aren't the ones with the flashiest demos. They're the ones who treated agent deployment like deploying any other production service. With monitoring, rollback plans, and operational runbooks.
tones.provocateur:
The AI agent space is 90% theater right now.

Someone spins up a LangChain demo, records a screen share, posts it with "the future is here," and collects 50K impressions. Meanwhile that agent would crash and burn in production within two days.

I know because I've been running real agents on real cron jobs for weeks. Nightly content generation, data enrichment, repo maintenance. And the thing that makes them work isn't the model or the framework. It's boring infrastructure that nobody wants to talk about.

Logging. Health checks. Alerting. Retry logic. Context window budgeting.

You know what killed my pipeline for 11 days? A dev server stopped. Not a model hallucination. Not a prompt failure. A process died and nothing told me.

The demo-to-production gap in AI agents isn't a technical problem. It's a cultural one. The community optimizes for impressions, not uptime. For wow factor, not operational maturity.

Every agent framework sells you autonomy. None of them sell you the ops layer that makes autonomy survivable. That's your job to build. And until you do, your agents are just expensive scripts with extra steps.

I've been running AI agents on cron jobs for a few weeks now. Nightly content generation, data enrichment, the kind of stuff that just ticks along in the background.

The thing nobody talks about in the agent hype cycle is how unglamorous production agents actually are. My pipeline died for 11 days once because a dev server stopped and I had no health checks. The fix wasn't a better model. It was a process monitor.

This week I want to break down what I've learned about keeping agents alive in production. Not the cool parts. The plumbing that actually matters. Logging, context budgeting, cost routing, and why your agent framework won't save you from operational debt.

posts.title: GTM engineer is the new full stack
posts.hook: Sales ops, rev ops, GTM engineer. Three titles, one job, zero agreement on what it actually means.
posts.body:
Sales ops, rev ops, GTM engineer. Three titles, one job, zero agreement on what it actually means.

I've been watching this role emerge from the inside. Started as an SDR doing manual list building in Salesforce. Now I write Python scripts that enrich leads through Clay, score them against ICP fit, and push qualified accounts into sequencing tools automatically.

The pattern I keep seeing: companies hire a "GTM engineer" expecting a marketer who can use Zapier. What they actually need is someone who can write SQL, build API integrations, and think in data pipelines.

The teams scaling outbound without adding headcount aren't doing it with better templates. They're doing it with better data architecture. Enrichment waterfall that hits 6 sources before a lead touches a sequence. Scoring models that actually reflect buying signals, not just firmographics.

This role sits at the intersection of engineering and revenue. And most job descriptions still don't capture it.
posts.cta: If you're building GTM infrastructure, what does your tech stack actually look like? Not the vendor logos. The actual data flow.

inspired_by.author: Sami Rahman
inspired_by.summary: Discusses building a GTM engineering function from scratch, focusing on integrating outbound automation tools to boost pipeline efficiency. Shares a framework for sales ops teams to automate prospecting without losing personalization. Emphasizes measurable ROI in revenue operations.

remixes.label: skill-stack-breakdown
remixes.hook: The highest-leverage role in B2B right now doesn't have a standard job description.
remixes.body:
The highest-leverage role in B2B right now doesn't have a standard job description.

I'm talking about the person who can write a Python script to enrich 500 leads through Clay, score them against ICP criteria, push qualified accounts into sequencing tools, and then explain the conversion data to the VP of Sales in a language they understand.

Two years ago this was three people. A sales ops manager configuring Salesforce. A data analyst pulling reports. A rev ops lead stitching tools together. Now one person with the right skill stack does all of it before lunch.

The skill stack looks like this: SQL for querying and joining data sources. Python for automation and API integrations. Basic understanding of sales methodology so you know what "qualified" actually means. Enough product sense to know which signals matter.

Companies keep posting these roles as "sales ops" and offering $65K. Then they wonder why they can't fill them. The person who can do this work is a software engineer who happens to understand revenue. That's not a $65K skill set.

The GTM engineer is the new full stack developer. And the market hasn't priced it in yet.
remixes.cta: What would you pay for someone who can build your entire lead-to-pipeline automation? Curious what the market thinks this is worth.

remixes.label: death-of-manual-ops
remixes.hook: I watched a sales team manually qualify 200 leads last week. It took four people three days. I built the same workflow in an afternoon.
remixes.body:
I watched a sales team manually qualify 200 leads last week. It took four people three days. I built the same workflow in an afternoon.

Not because I'm faster. Because the workflow was already solved. Pull company data from enrichment APIs. Score against ICP criteria. Filter out bad fits. Route qualified accounts to the right rep. It's a pipeline, not a project.

The problem is organizational, not technical. Sales teams still think in terms of reps and territories. They hire humans to do data plumbing because that's how the org chart has always worked.

Meanwhile, one person with Python, an API key, and a clear ICP definition can replace an entire manual qualification process. Not with AI magic. With basic automation that's been possible for years but nobody in sales leadership knew how to ask for.

This is why the GTM engineer role is exploding. Companies are realizing they don't need five SDRs doing manual research. They need one technical person who understands the revenue motion and can automate the repetitive parts.

The shift isn't AI replacing salespeople. It's engineering absorbing sales ops. And the people who straddle both worlds are about to be very well compensated.
remixes.cta: How much of your sales team's time is spent on work that could be a script? I'd bet it's more than you think.

First, define the role clearly. This is not sales ops with a new title. GTM engineers need to write code, understand data pipelines, and think in systems. Hire accordingly.

posts.title: the compute cost nobody talks about
posts.hook: I track my daily AI compute costs in a SQLite database. Most days it's under $3.
posts.body:
I track my daily AI compute costs in a SQLite database. Most days it's under $3.

That number surprises people because the narrative is that AI is expensive. And it can be, if you're running Opus on every classification task or sending entire codebases through context windows for trivial lookups.

My stack right now:

- Qwen 2.5 14B running locally on Ollama for high-frequency cron tasks. Free.
- Claude Sonnet for conversational workflows. Cheap.
- Claude Opus for content generation and complex reasoning. Used sparingly.

The trick isn't picking one model. It's routing intelligently. The nightly blog generator uses Opus because quality matters there. The Reddit scout that checks 50 subreddits uses a local model because speed and cost matter more than prose quality.

Most people either go all-in on the most expensive model or refuse to pay for any API calls. The middle ground, where you match model capability to task complexity, is where the economics actually work.

Track your costs. You'll be surprised what's cheap and what's burning money for no reason.
posts.cta: Anyone else running a mixed local + API model stack? What's your routing logic look like?

inspired_by.author: Andrej Karpathy
inspired_by.summary: Explores advancements in AI infrastructure for training large models, focusing on efficient compute scaling and new hardware like TPUs. Predicts how this will accelerate agent-based automation in software development. Calls for open-source tools to democratize access.

remixes.label: model-routing-playbook
remixes.hook: Running Opus on every task is like taking a helicopter to the grocery store. It works, but your economics are broken.
remixes.body:
Running Opus on every task is like taking a helicopter to the grocery store. It works, but your economics are broken.

I route AI tasks across four different models depending on what the job actually needs:

Qwen 2.5 14B runs locally on Ollama. Free. Handles high-frequency cron tasks like classification, tagging, and data formatting. It's not the smartest model. It doesn't need to be.

Claude Sonnet handles conversational work and mid-complexity tasks. Good balance of speed and quality for the price.

Claude Opus gets the hard stuff. Content creation, complex reasoning, code architecture decisions. The tasks where quality directly impacts outcomes.

The routing logic is simple: if a task is repetitive and structured, go local. If it needs real reasoning, go API. If it's mission-critical, go frontier.

Most days my total compute cost is under $3. Not because I'm skimping. Because 80% of my agent tasks genuinely don't need a frontier model.

The expensive AI narrative comes from people who default to the biggest model for everything. That's not a technology problem. It's an architecture problem. Route your models like you'd route your microservices. Match the capability to the requirement.
remixes.cta: What's your model routing strategy? Single model for everything, or do you split by task type?

remixes.label: receipts-over-narratives
remixes.hook: Everyone has opinions about AI costs. I have a database.
remixes.body:
Everyone has opinions about AI costs. I have a database.

I've been logging every API call, token count, and model selection into a SQLite table since January. Not because I'm obsessive. Because I wanted actual data instead of vibes about what AI infrastructure costs.

The results surprised me.

Most days: under $3 total. Some days with heavy content generation: $8-12. The monthly average sits around $90.

For that $90 I get: nightly content generation across multiple formats, lead enrichment and scoring for hundreds of accounts, automated repo maintenance, daily blog posts, and a handful of ad-hoc coding sessions.

The breakdown by model is revealing. Local Ollama handles about 60% of total tasks at zero marginal cost. Sonnet handles another 25%. Opus, the expensive one, only touches maybe 15% of workloads. But that 15% is where all the quality-sensitive output lives.

The takeaway isn't that AI is cheap. It's that AI costs are controllable if you treat model selection as an engineering decision instead of a default. Most people pick one model and use it for everything. That's like running every database query against your production primary. It works until the bill arrives.
remixes.cta: Do you track your AI spend at the task level? If not, you might be surprised where the money actually goes.

tones.advisor:
If you're running AI workloads in production, your model routing strategy is your single biggest cost lever. Here's how I'd approach it.

I track daily compute costs in a SQLite database. Most days the total is under $3. That number is achievable for anyone willing to be deliberate about model selection.

My recommended stack architecture:

Tier 1, local models for high-frequency structured tasks. I run Qwen 2.5 14B on Ollama. Zero marginal cost. Handles classification, formatting, tagging, and anything with predictable input/output shapes.

Tier 2, mid-range API models for conversational and moderate-complexity work. Sonnet handles most interactive tasks well. Good quality-to-cost ratio.

Tier 3, frontier models for quality-critical output only. Opus for content creation, complex code architecture, and anything where the output directly faces customers or stakeholders.

The routing logic should be: start every task at the cheapest tier that can handle it. Escalate only when quality requirements demand it.

Most organizations overspend on AI because they default to frontier models for everything. That's a solvable architecture problem. Audit your workloads, classify them by complexity, and route accordingly. You'll likely cut costs by 60-70% without any quality degradation on the tasks that matter.
tones.provocateur:
The "AI is expensive" narrative is a skill issue.

I track every dollar of AI compute in a SQLite database. Most days it's under $3. And I'm running content generation, lead enrichment, repo maintenance, and daily blog posts. Not toy projects.

So when I see founders complaining about $500/month API bills, I already know what happened. They pointed Opus at every task like it's the only model that exists. Classification tasks that a 7B local model handles fine? Opus. Formatting JSON? Opus. Checking if a string contains an email? Believe it or not, Opus.

This isn't an AI cost problem. It's an engineering laziness problem.

I run Qwen 2.5 locally for 60% of my workloads. Free. Sonnet handles another 25%. Opus only touches the 15% where frontier capability actually changes the output quality.

The people spending $500/month could probably get the same results for $50 with basic model routing. But that requires thinking about your architecture instead of just throwing tokens at the wall.

AI infrastructure has the same economics as cloud infrastructure. The default configuration is always the most expensive one. The savings come from people who bother to optimize.

posts.title: building in public without the performance
posts.hook: Building in public has a credibility problem. Too many "day 47 of my journey" posts that are really just content calendars disguised as transparency.
posts.body:
Building in public has a credibility problem. Too many "day 47 of my journey" posts that are really just content calendars disguised as transparency.

Real building in public looks like this:

My agent pipeline died for 11 days because a dev server stopped running and I didn't have health checks. Didn't notice until the database stopped updating. The fix was a launchd plist with KeepAlive set to true. Five lines of XML.

That's not a sexy post. But it's real.

The founders I respect most share the plumbing, not just the milestones. Pieter Levels talks about actual server configs. Sahil Lavingia shares real revenue numbers, including the flat months.

The pattern that works: share the specific thing you built, the specific problem it solved, and the specific thing that broke. Skip the narrative arc. Skip the "and that's when I realized" moment.

People can smell the difference between documentation and performance. Document what you're actually doing. The audience that matters will find you.
posts.cta: What's the most useful "building in public" post you've seen? Not the most viral. The most useful.

inspired_by.author: Arvid Kahl
inspired_by.summary: Arvid recounts pivoting from corporate job to full-time indie hacker, building and selling his first SaaS product FeedbackPanda for $200k+. He advises on validating ideas quickly and building in public to gain early users and feedback. The post includes a step-by-step guide for others considering a tech career pivot.

remixes.label: failure-log-format
remixes.hook: My most useful "building in public" content is a list of things that broke and how long it took me to notice.
remixes.body:
My most useful "building in public" content is a list of things that broke and how long it took me to notice.

Not engagement metrics. Not revenue screenshots. A failure log.

Here's a real one: my agent pipeline died on February 25th. A dev server process stopped. No health checks. No alerts. I didn't notice until March 8th when I wondered why the database hadn't updated in a while. Eleven days of silent failure.

The fix took 20 minutes. A launchd plist with KeepAlive set to true. That's it.

Another one: I shipped a content pipeline that published AI-generated posts without a human approval gate. The third post was mediocre enough that someone DM'd me asking if I was okay. Added the approval step that afternoon.

These posts don't go viral. They don't get the "so inspiring" comments. But they're the ones that actually help other builders, because everyone has the same failures and nobody talks about them.

The building in public format that works isn't a highlight reel. It's: here's what broke, here's how long I was oblivious, here's the fix, here's what I'd monitor differently next time.

Four sentences. More useful than a 30-day journey thread.
remixes.cta: What's your best failure story from building something? The ones that feel embarrassing are usually the most helpful.

remixes.label: content-calendar-callout
remixes.hook: If your "building in public" posts are scheduled three weeks in advance, you're not building in public. You're running a content calendar.
remixes.body:
If your "building in public" posts are scheduled three weeks in advance, you're not building in public. You're running a content calendar.

There's nothing wrong with content calendars. But calling them "building in public" is dishonest, and the audience can tell.

Real building in public has specific qualities:

It's reactive, not planned. Something happened today, and you're sharing it while it's fresh.

It includes the embarrassing parts. Not "we pivoted our strategy" but "I shipped a bug that broke the site for 6 hours and nobody told me because nobody was using it yet."

It has specific technical details. Not "we improved our infrastructure" but "I added a KeepAlive flag to a launchd plist because my server kept dying and I had zero monitoring."

It doesn't always have a neat lesson. Sometimes the post is just "this thing broke and I'm still figuring out why."

The building in public posts I actually bookmark are the ones that make me feel less alone in the chaos. Not the ones that make someone's journey look inevitable.

The bar is low. Just be honest about what happened today. That's the whole format.
remixes.cta: Drop a link to a building-in-public post that actually helped you. Not impressed you. Helped you.

tones.builder:

My agent pipeline died for 11 days because a dev server stopped running and I didn't have health checks. Didn't notice until the database stopped updating. The fix was a launchd plist with KeepAlive set to true. Five lines of XML.

That's not a sexy post. But it's real.

The founders I respect most share the plumbing, not just the milestones. Pieter Levels talks about actual server configs. Sahil Lavingia shares real revenue numbers, including the flat months.

tones.advisor:
If you're building in public, here's how to make it actually valuable instead of performative.

The current state of building in public content is mostly theater. Journey posts that are pre-written. Milestone celebrations that skip the messy middle. Day-count threads that are really just content marketing.

Here's what I recommend instead:

Share failures with specific details. My agent pipeline died for 11 days because a dev server stopped and I had no health checks. I didn't notice until the database went stale. The fix was a launchd plist with KeepAlive. That level of specificity helps people. "We had infrastructure challenges" does not.

Include the time-to-detection, not just the fix. Knowing something broke is useful. Knowing it broke and you didn't notice for 11 days is more useful, because it normalizes the reality that monitoring gaps happen to everyone.

Skip the narrative arc. Not everything needs a lesson. Sometimes the most honest post is: this broke, here's what I changed, I'm not sure it's fully fixed yet.

Post reactively, not on schedule. If your building-in-public content can be written three weeks ahead, it's not building in public. It's content marketing with a transparency wrapper.

The audience for real building-in-public content is smaller but significantly more engaged. Optimize for usefulness, not reach.

90% of "building in public" is a lie. And everyone knows it.

Day 47 of my journey. Day 62. Day 100. Beautiful graphics. Perfectly timed milestones. Engagement pods in the replies. Zero useful information for anyone actually trying to build something.

You know what real building in public looks like? My agent pipeline died for 11 days and I didn't notice. Why? No health checks. No monitoring. I was so focused on capabilities that I forgot the basics. The fix took 20 minutes. The embarrassment lasted longer.

That post won't get 50K impressions. It won't land me a podcast appearance. But it might save someone else from making the same mistake.

The building-in-public creators with the biggest audiences are often the least honest. Because honesty doesn't optimize for engagement. "I shipped a feature and it worked great" gets hearts. "I shipped a feature that broke silently for a week and a half" gets uncomfortable silence.

But the uncomfortable silence posts are the ones that actually help people.

If your building-in-public content could be written by a social media intern who's never touched your codebase, you're not sharing what you're building. You're performing a character.

posts.title: hybrid human-AI is the only honest answer
posts.hook: Fully autonomous AI agents are a fantasy for 90% of business workflows right now. And that's fine.
posts.body:
Fully autonomous AI agents are a fantasy for 90% of business workflows right now. And that's fine.

I run a content pipeline where Claude generates drafts, but every piece goes through a human approval gate before publishing. The AI handles the volume. I handle the judgment calls.

The posts that perform best? They start as AI drafts that I tear apart and rebuild. The structure comes from the model. The specificity, the voice, the "actually I burned 6 hours on this" details come from me.

Teams that go full-auto on content get caught within a week. The patterns are obvious. Em-dashes everywhere, dramatic rhetorical questions, that weird bookend thing where the conclusion restates the introduction.

Teams that refuse to use AI at all ship at half the pace.

The sweet spot is boring: AI generates the first pass, human applies taste and judgment, AI handles distribution and formatting. It's not a clean narrative. It's not "AI replaces writers" or "AI can't write." It's a messy collaboration that produces better output than either alone.

Most real solutions are messy like that.
posts.cta: Where do you draw the human/AI line in your workflows? Curious where people's trust boundaries actually sit.

remixes.label: approval-gate-architecture
remixes.hook: My AI writes the first draft. I decide if it ships. That division of labor is the whole strategy.
remixes.body:
My AI writes the first draft. I decide if it ships. That division of labor is the whole strategy.

I run a content pipeline where Claude generates drafts on a nightly cron. Blog posts, social content, data summaries. Every morning I have a queue of finished drafts waiting for review.

Some I publish with minor edits. Some I rewrite almost entirely, keeping only the structure. Some I kill on sight.

The approval gate isn't a limitation. It's the architecture.

Here's what the AI handles well: volume, consistency, structure, first-draft speed, and coverage of topics I'd never get to manually. It turns "I should write about that" into an actual draft instead of a note in my backlog.

Here's what I handle: voice, judgment, specificity, knowing which details make a piece credible vs. generic, and the final call on whether something meets the bar.

The posts that perform best aren't pure AI or pure human. They're AI structure with human edge. The model builds the skeleton. I add the scars and calluses that make it real.

People keep framing this as AI vs. human. The actual unlock is AI then human. Sequential, not competitive. Each doing the part they're best at.
remixes.cta: What's your approval process for AI-generated content? Or are you shipping raw outputs?

remixes.label: trust-boundary-map
remixes.hook: I trust AI with my first drafts but not my published words. I trust it with data enrichment but not deal decisions. The line is clearer than people think.
remixes.body:
I trust AI with my first drafts but not my published words. I trust it with data enrichment but not deal decisions. The line is clearer than people think.

After months of running hybrid workflows, I've mapped where the trust boundary actually sits:

High trust (let it run autonomously): data formatting, enrichment, classification, scheduling, repo maintenance, code generation with tests.

Medium trust (review before action): content drafts, email drafts, lead scoring, summarization of source material.

Low trust (human decides, AI assists): publishing decisions, outbound messaging, pricing, anything that touches a customer relationship directly.

The pattern is simple. The more the output is visible to other humans, the more human review it needs. Internal automation can run hot. External-facing output needs a gate.

Most people I talk to are at one extreme. Either they don't trust AI at all, so they're doing everything manually. Or they over-trust it, shipping raw model output to customers and wondering why it feels off.

The middle path works. Let AI handle the volume and the grind. Keep humans on the judgment calls and the quality bar. Build explicit gates between the two.

Full autonomy is a goal for maybe 10% of workflows. Hybrid is the strategy for the other 90%.
remixes.cta: Where's your trust boundary? What does AI do autonomously in your workflow vs. what still needs a human eye?

tones.advisor:
For most business workflows today, the right AI strategy is hybrid, not autonomous. Here's how I'd structure it.

I run a content pipeline where Claude generates drafts, but every piece passes through a human approval gate before publishing. The AI handles volume. I handle judgment.

My recommended framework for deciding what to automate vs. what to keep human:

Automate fully: repetitive data work, formatting, classification, scheduling, anything with clear input/output specs and low cost of error.

Automate with review: content drafts, communications, scoring, summarization. The AI does the heavy lifting, a human checks the output before it goes anywhere visible.

Keep human: final publishing decisions, customer-facing communications, pricing, relationship management. Anywhere the cost of a wrong output is high.

The key architectural principle: build explicit approval gates between AI generation and external-facing output. Don't rely on the model getting it right. Assume it will occasionally miss, and design your workflow so that miss gets caught.

The posts in my pipeline that perform best start as AI drafts that I substantially rework. The model provides structure and coverage. I provide voice and specificity. This sequential approach, AI then human, consistently outperforms either one working alone.

Don't aim for full automation. Aim for the right handoff points.
tones.provocateur:
The "fully autonomous AI" crowd is selling you a fantasy. And they know it.

Every AI influencer posting about their "100% automated" content pipeline is either lying about the human review they do behind the scenes, or they're publishing garbage and haven't checked their engagement metrics lately.

I run AI-generated content daily. And every single piece goes through a human approval gate. Not because the AI is bad. Because judgment is the product, not the text.

The drafts that perform best? They start as AI output that I tear apart. I keep the structure. I rewrite the voice. I add the specific details that make it credible instead of generic.

That's not a limitation. That's the architecture working correctly.

Full autonomy makes sense for maybe 10% of workflows. Data formatting, classification, repo maintenance. Stuff where wrong is detectable and fixable.

For everything else, the honest answer is hybrid. AI does the volume. Humans do the judgment. Anyone telling you otherwise is either selling something or not shipping real work.

The future isn't AI replacing human judgment. It's AI making human judgment scalable. Big difference.

posts.title: outbound is a data problem now
posts.hook: The outbound teams still sending the same sequence to every lead on a list are competing against teams running 6-source enrichment waterfalls before a single email goes out.
posts.body:
The outbound teams still sending the same sequence to every lead on a list are competing against teams running 6-source enrichment waterfalls before a single email goes out.

This isn't a fair fight.

I built an enrichment pipeline that checks Apollo, Clay, LinkedIn, company websites, job postings, and technographic data before qualifying a lead. By the time someone enters a sequence, I know their tech stack, recent hires, funding stage, and which pain points to lead with.

The sequence isn't personalized because I wrote custom copy for each person. It's personalized because the data tells me which message framework to use.

Company just raised Series B and hired 3 SDRs? They're scaling outbound. Lead with efficiency.
Company has 200 employees and no rev ops hire? They're doing it manually. Lead with automation.

This is what GTM engineering actually looks like. Not better subject lines. Better signal detection.

The teams adding headcount to scale outbound are solving a data problem with bodies. The teams building enrichment infrastructure are solving it with leverage.
posts.cta: What enrichment sources are actually worth paying for in your experience? I've found huge variance in data quality across providers.

remixes.label: buyer-psychology-flip
remixes.hook: Your prospects can tell when you didn't do your homework. The email reads different when you actually know their stack.
remixes.body:
Your prospects can tell when you didn't do your homework. The email reads different when you actually know their stack.

I stopped thinking about outbound as a volume problem and started treating it as a research problem. Before anyone on my list gets an email, I already know their tech stack from job postings, their growth signals from hiring pages, their org structure from LinkedIn, and their pain points from their own company blog.

The result isn't just higher reply rates. It's different conversations. When your first email references a specific challenge they're actually facing, you skip the "who are you and why should I care" phase entirely.

Most outbound tools sell you on send volume. More emails, more sequences, more touches. But the math doesn't work that way anymore. Spam filters got smarter. Buyers got more skeptical. The teams winning right now aren't sending more. They're sending less, with more signal packed into every message.

I run a 6-source enrichment waterfall. Apollo for contact data, Clay for company signals, LinkedIn for org context, job boards for hiring intent, technographic scrapers for stack info, and company websites for messaging cues. It takes longer per lead. The pipeline is smaller. But the conversion math destroys batch-and-blast every time.
remixes.cta: What's the one data source that actually moved your reply rates? Not theoretically. Actually.

remixes.label: death-of-spray-and-pray
remixes.hook: I disqualify 70% of leads before they ever see a sequence. That's not a bug in my pipeline. That's the whole point.
remixes.body:
I disqualify 70% of leads before they ever see a sequence. That's not a bug in my pipeline. That's the whole point.

Most outbound orgs measure activity. Emails sent, calls made, sequences launched. Then they wonder why reply rates keep dropping. The answer is simple: your leads can smell a mass email. They've seen your template before. They know you didn't check if they even use the category of tool you're selling.

I built a qualification layer that runs before sequencing. It pulls from six data sources, scores fit based on real signals, not just firmographics, and only passes through leads where I have enough context to write something worth reading.

The counterintuitive part: working fewer leads made the pipeline bigger. Not because of some magic. Because reps stopped burning time on accounts that were never going to convert. Because reply rates tripled when messages were actually relevant. Because the sales cycle shortened when the first touch demonstrated real understanding.

Outbound isn't dying. Lazy outbound is dying. The teams investing in data infrastructure before send infrastructure are pulling ahead fast.
remixes.cta: How much of your current sequence list would survive a real qualification filter? Honest answers only.

posts.title: code review agents are overhyped
posts.hook: AI code review tools catch syntax issues and style violations. They miss the thing that actually matters: whether the approach is right.
posts.body:
AI code review tools catch syntax issues and style violations. They miss the thing that actually matters: whether the approach is right.

I use Claude Code daily. It's genuinely good at implementation. Give it a clear spec and it writes solid code. But when I let it review its own architectural decisions without guardrails, it optimizes for patterns it's seen before, not patterns that fit this specific system.

Last week it suggested adding an abstraction layer for something I'll only use once. Clean code by textbook standards. Over-engineering by production standards. Three similar lines of code would have been better than a premature abstraction.

The dev tools that actually save me time:

- AI for first-draft implementation and boilerplate
- AI for finding files and understanding unfamiliar codebases
- Human judgment for "should we even build this" and "is this the right shape"
- AI for reformatting, refactoring within a decided architecture

The pattern: AI is great at executing within constraints. It's mediocre at setting the constraints. The tools that lean into that distinction are the ones that stick.
posts.cta: What's one AI dev tool that actually changed how you work, not just how you demo?

inspired_by.author: Nat Friedman
inspired_by.summary: Highlights emerging developer tools powered by AI agents that automate code review and testing pipelines. Shares examples from recent integrations in VS Code extensions. Stresses impact on productivity for engineering teams.

remixes.label: guardrails-over-reviews
remixes.hook: I stopped asking AI to review code. I started building systems that constrain what it can write in the first place.
remixes.body:
I stopped asking AI to review code. I started building systems that constrain what it can write in the first place.

The code review discourse is stuck on the wrong question. Everyone's asking "can AI catch bugs?" when they should be asking "can AI evaluate tradeoffs?" Those are fundamentally different problems.

Bug detection is pattern matching. AI is great at it. But the decisions that actually tank a project aren't bugs. They're architectural choices that look fine in isolation but compound into debt over months. Using an ORM when raw queries would keep things simpler. Adding a message queue for a job that runs once a day. Reaching for microservices when a monolith would ship in half the time.

I use Claude Code daily. When I give it a clear spec and explicit constraints, the output is solid. When I let it make architectural calls without boundaries, it gravitates toward whatever pattern shows up most in its training data. Popular isn't the same as correct for your specific context.

So I changed the approach. Instead of reviewing AI output after the fact, I write detailed specs upfront. File structure, data flow, what NOT to use. The constraints go in before the code comes out. Review becomes verification, not correction.

The real skill isn't prompting better. It's specifying better.
remixes.cta: What constraints do you set before letting AI write code? Or are you still reviewing everything after the fact?

remixes.label: expertise-gap-expose
remixes.hook: AI code review has the same problem as junior code review: it can't evaluate what it hasn't built before.
remixes.body:
AI code review has the same problem as junior code review: it can't evaluate what it hasn't built before.

A junior dev can spot a missing null check. They can flag inconsistent naming. They can catch an unused import. But ask them whether this service should be synchronous or event-driven, and they'll defer to whatever the codebase already does.

AI review tools work the same way. They're excellent at surface-level analysis. Syntax, style, common anti-patterns. They'll catch the easy stuff reliably. But they can't tell you that your database schema is going to create a nightmare when you need to add multi-tenancy in six months. They don't understand your deployment constraints, your team's skill gaps, or the business context that makes one approach better than another.

I ship code with Claude Code every day. It writes solid implementations. But I treat its architectural suggestions the same way I'd treat a junior's: interesting input, not final authority. The review that matters is whether the approach fits this specific system, this specific team, this specific set of constraints.

The tools that actually changed my workflow aren't the ones that review code. They're the ones that write code faster so I have more time for the review that only a human can do.
remixes.cta: Where do you draw the line between "AI can review this" and "a human needs to evaluate this"?

AI code review tools catch syntax issues and style violations. They miss the thing that actually matters: whether the approach is right.

Here's how I'd frame this for any team evaluating these tools. AI review is excellent at a specific layer of quality: formatting, common bugs, security anti-patterns, test coverage gaps. Invest in it for that. But don't mistake it for architectural review.

I use Claude Code daily, and the pattern I recommend is clear separation of concerns. Let AI handle implementation. Let it write code from detailed specs. Then apply human review at the decision layer. Is this the right abstraction? Does this data model support where the product is heading? Are we introducing coupling that'll slow us down later?

The teams getting burned are the ones treating AI review as a replacement for senior engineering judgment. It's not. It's a productivity multiplier for the mechanical parts of review, freeing up your senior people to focus on the architectural questions that actually determine whether a system succeeds or fails.

My recommendation: define which review categories you trust AI with, and which require a human. Make that boundary explicit in your review process. Don't leave it ambiguous.

Here's : most teams don't have great code review to begin with. Their human reviews are drive-by approvals. "LGTM" with no meaningful feedback. So they bolt on AI review and celebrate that something is finally leaving comments. But those comments are the equivalent of a spell checker on a bad essay. Technically correct. Strategically useless.

I use Claude Code daily. It writes better code than most mid-level devs I've worked with. But it has zero concept of whether this system should exist in the first place. It'll happily build an elegant solution to a problem you created by making bad architectural choices three sprints ago.

The hype around AI code review is a symptom of a bigger problem: teams want to automate judgment because judgment is expensive and slow. But you can't automate taste. You can't automate the pattern recognition that comes from watching three projects fail the same way. You can't train a model on "this will be a maintenance nightmare in 18 months."

Stop looking for AI to replace your senior engineers. Start using it to make them faster.

I use Claude Code daily. Great at implementation. But without guardrails, it optimizes for patterns it's seen, not patterns that fit your system.

Write better specs. Skip the AI review theater.
platforms.newsletter:
I want to talk about something the AI dev tool discourse keeps getting wrong.

Everyone's excited about AI code review. And the tools are genuinely good at catching surface-level issues. Style violations, common bugs, missing edge cases. Real value there.

But the reviews that actually prevent disasters aren't about syntax. They're about approach. Is this the right abstraction? Does this schema support where we're heading? Should this be a service or a library?

AI can't answer those questions yet. I use Claude Code every day and I've learned exactly where to trust it and where to override it. That boundary is the skill that matters now.

posts.title: the solo builder advantage is speed
posts.hook: A solo builder with AI tools ships faster than a 5-person team with a sprint planning meeting.
posts.body:
A solo builder with AI tools ships faster than a 5-person team with a sprint planning meeting.

Not because the code is better. Because the decision loop is instant.

I run a monorepo with 3 Next.js sites, a Python automation layer, cron jobs, a CRM integration pipeline, and a content distribution system. One person. The AI handles implementation velocity. I handle direction.

What makes this work isn't talent. It's elimination of coordination overhead.

No standup to explain what I'm doing. No PR review queue. No Jira ticket to justify a 20-minute fix. I see a bug, I fix it, I push it. The feedback loop between "I want this" and "this exists" is measured in minutes, not sprints.

The tradeoff is real though. No second pair of eyes means mistakes ship to production. My pre-push hook that scans for sensitive data exists because I almost pushed client names to a public repo. Twice.

Solo building with AI isn't about being a 10x engineer. It's about having a 0.1x coordination cost. The math works out even if your code is average.
posts.cta: Solo builders: what's your biggest "oh no" moment from not having a code review process?

inspired_by.author: Pieter Levels
inspired_by.summary: Pieter shares his journey as a solo founder building and shipping multiple products like Nomad List without VC funding. He emphasizes the power of building in public, iterating based on user feedback, and achieving profitability quickly. His post details the tech stack and lessons from launching 12 startups in 12 months.

remixes.label: decision-loop-breakdown
remixes.hook: The fastest part of my stack isn't the AI. It's the fact that I don't need a meeting to change direction.
remixes.body:
The fastest part of my stack isn't the AI. It's the fact that I don't need a meeting to change direction.

I noticed a bug in my CRM integration pipeline at 10 PM on a Tuesday. Diagnosed it, fixed it, deployed it, and confirmed the fix in production. Total time: 23 minutes. No Slack thread. No Jira ticket. No standup update. No PR review queue.

This isn't about being better than a team. It's about removing the latency that exists between every decision point in a team workflow. Every handoff, every review, every approval. Each one adds hours or days. Multiply that across a real codebase and the compound effect is massive.

I run 3 Next.js sites, a Python automation layer, cron pipelines, CRM integrations, and a content distribution system. The AI handles implementation speed. I handle the decisions that require context no model has: what to build, what to skip, what matters this week versus next quarter.

The tradeoff is real. No safety net. No second pair of eyes. I mitigate that with aggressive CI checks, automated tests on critical paths, and a rule that if a fix feels hacky, I stop and rethink before shipping.

But the speed advantage isn't marginal. It's structural. And it compounds every single day.
remixes.cta: What's the longest you've waited for a decision that could have been made in 5 minutes? What did that delay actually cost?

remixes.label: anti-team-myth
remixes.hook: "You can't build real infrastructure alone." I hear this from people who've never tried it with modern tools.
remixes.body:
"You can't build real infrastructure alone." I hear this from people who've never tried it with modern tools.

Let me describe what "alone" looks like in 2026. I write specs. Claude Code writes implementations. I review, adjust, deploy. The cycle time between idea and production is measured in minutes, not sprints.

My current stack: a monorepo with 3 production Next.js sites, a Python automation layer that handles content distribution and data enrichment, cron jobs running on a single Mac Mini, CRM integrations pushing data between systems, and an AI assistant with persistent memory that evolves over time.

One person built and maintains all of it.

But here's what gets lost in the "solo builder" narrative: this only works because I'm deliberate about what I don't build. I don't write custom auth. I don't build my own database. I don't write CSS frameworks. Every dependency I don't maintain is bandwidth I keep for the parts that actually differentiate.

The solo advantage isn't about doing everything. It's about having zero coordination overhead on the things you do. No sprint planning. No architecture review boards. No waiting for DevOps to approve your deployment.

The limiting factor isn't capability anymore. It's taste and prioritization.
remixes.cta: Solo builders: what's the thing you explicitly decided NOT to build that saved you the most time?

posts.title: vector DBs are the wrong default
posts.hook: Not every AI application needs a vector database. Most of mine use SQLite with full-text search.
posts.body:
Not every AI application needs a vector database. Most of mine use SQLite with full-text search.

I store conversation memory, initiative tracking, XP systems, and content metadata in a single SQLite file. FTS5 handles keyword search. For the rare semantic lookup, I query the LLM directly with the relevant context loaded.

The production AI stack I see recommended everywhere: vector DB, embedding pipeline, retrieval layer, reranking model, orchestration framework. That's 5 moving parts before you've written any business logic.

My stack: SQLite, a Python script, and an API call. It's been running for months.

Vector DBs make sense when you have thousands of documents and need fuzzy semantic matching. But most people reaching for Pinecone have 50 documents and would be fine with a grep.

The infrastructure decisions that actually matter for production AI:

- Persistence (where does state live between runs)
- Error recovery (what happens when the API is down at 2am)
- Cost routing (which model handles which task)

None of those require a vector DB. They require thinking about your system as a system, not as a collection of trending tools.
posts.cta: What's the simplest infrastructure decision you made that you're glad you didn't over-engineer?

inspired_by.author: Eliot Andres
inspired_by.summary: Breaks down building production-grade AI agents with custom infrastructure stacks, including vector DBs and orchestration layers. Provides tips on monitoring and debugging agent failures. Discusses trade-offs in latency vs. capability.

remixes.label: complexity-trap-warning
remixes.hook: I watched a startup spend two months integrating Pinecone for a feature that could have been 30 lines of SQLite. They called it "AI infrastructure." I'd call it resume-driven development.
remixes.body:
I watched a startup spend two months integrating Pinecone for a feature that could have been 30 lines of SQLite. They called it "AI infrastructure." I'd call it resume-driven development.

The vector database hype has a specific failure mode: teams adopt the tool before they have the problem. They read a blog post about RAG pipelines, spin up a vector DB, build an embedding pipeline, and then discover their actual use case is keyword search over 10,000 records.

I store conversation memory, initiative tracking, an XP system, and content metadata in a single SQLite file. FTS5 handles keyword search. When I need semantic lookup, I load the relevant context and query the LLM directly. No embeddings. No vector store. No separate infrastructure to monitor.

The production cost of this approach: zero. The operational complexity: one file I can copy, backup, or inspect with a CLI tool. The performance: instant for every query I've thrown at it.

The vector DB makes sense when you have millions of documents, when semantic similarity is the primary access pattern, and when keyword search genuinely fails. That's a real use case. But it's not the default use case. Most AI applications I see in production are searching structured data with known query patterns. SQLite with FTS5 handles that better and simpler.

Pick your infrastructure based on your actual access patterns. Not the conference talk you saw last month.
remixes.cta: What's the most over-engineered piece of your current stack? The thing that could be replaced with something boring?

remixes.label: pragmatic-stack-reveal
remixes.hook: My entire AI memory system runs on a single SQLite file. No vector DB. No embedding pipeline. No managed service. Here's why that's a feature, not a limitation.
remixes.body:
My entire AI memory system runs on a single SQLite file. No vector DB. No embedding pipeline. No managed service. Here's why that's a feature, not a limitation.

I track conversation history, initiative backlog, XP progression, daily costs, and content metadata in one database. Every query is instant. Backups are a file copy. Debugging is a sqlite3 CLI session. The entire thing runs on a Mac Mini with zero external dependencies.

For search, I use FTS5. Full-text search that's built into SQLite. It handles keyword matching across all my tables. For the 5% of cases where I need semantic understanding, I load the relevant rows as context and let the LLM interpret. No embedding step. No similarity threshold tuning. No index rebuilding.

The boring infrastructure advantages compound. When something breaks at 2 AM, I'm debugging a single file, not a distributed system. When I want to test a new feature, I copy the DB file and experiment without risking production data. When I need to understand what happened last Tuesday, I write a SQL query, not a vector similarity search that returns "maybe relevant" results.

Vector databases solve a real problem. But the problem they solve is "I have too much unstructured data to search with keywords." If that's not your problem, you're adding complexity for architecture diagram aesthetics.
remixes.cta: What's running in production for you on infrastructure that most people would call "too simple"? Those are usually the most reliable parts of the stack.

Here's how I'd evaluate this decision for your stack. Ask two questions: What's your data volume? And what's your primary access pattern?

If you're searching fewer than a million documents and your queries map to known keywords, categories, or structured fields, SQLite with FTS5 will outperform a vector DB in speed, cost, and operational simplicity. I've run this in production for months. Conversation memory, initiative tracking, content metadata. All in one file.

Vector databases are the right choice when you have genuinely unstructured data at scale and similarity search is the primary access pattern. Think: searching a corpus of 10 million documents where users don't know the exact terms. That's a real use case.

But most AI applications I see in production are doing structured queries with predictable patterns. For those, my recommendation: start with SQLite. Add FTS5 for text search. Use the LLM for semantic interpretation when needed by loading context directly. You can always migrate to a vector DB later if your access patterns demand it. You can't easily undo the operational complexity once it's in your stack.

But that's not what the AI infrastructure vendors want you to hear. They need you to believe that every chatbot requires an embedding pipeline, a vector store, and a managed service with a monthly bill. Because that's how they make money.

I store conversation memory, initiative tracking, XP systems, and content metadata in a single SQLite file. Total infrastructure cost: zero. Operational overhead: none. Performance: faster than any managed vector DB I've tested, because the data is local and the queries are simple.

The vector DB hype follows the same pattern as every infrastructure fad. NoSQL was going to replace relational databases. Microservices were going to replace monoliths. Kubernetes was going to make deployment simple. Each one solved a real problem for a small number of teams. Then the conference circuit turned it into a default choice for everyone.

FTS5 handles 95% of my search needs. For the remaining 5%, I load context into the LLM prompt directly. No embeddings. No cosine similarity. No index management. Just the data and the model.

The most powerful infrastructure choice in 2026 is saying "no, we don't need that yet."

posts.title: content strategy for revenue teams is broken
posts.hook: Revenue teams create content to "build pipeline." Then measure it by impressions. That's not a strategy. That's a vanity loop.
posts.body:
Revenue teams create content to "build pipeline." Then measure it by impressions. That's not a strategy. That's a vanity loop.

I distribute every piece of content across LinkedIn, X, Reddit, and a blog. Each platform gets a rewrite for that audience. Each post links back to owned property. Every engagement gets tracked.

The thing most rev ops content strategies miss: distribution is the strategy. The writing is 30% of the work. Reformatting for 4 platforms, engaging in comments, responding to replies, cross-posting at the right times. That's the other 70%.

I automated most of it. A script reads my blog post, generates platform-specific drafts, and queues them in Typefully. Another script scouts Reddit threads where the topic is already being discussed. A third tracks which keywords are driving organic traffic.

The content itself is straightforward. Tactical, specific, helpful. No thought leadership. No frameworks with branded names. Just "here's what I built, here's how it works, here's the config."

Teams that align content to actual pipeline don't measure impressions. They measure replies, DMs, and inbound that references specific posts. The data is harder to collect but impossible to fake.
posts.cta: Revenue teams doing content well: what's your actual attribution model? Not the deck version. The real one.

inspired_by.author: Amy Volas
inspired_by.summary: Outlines a content strategy playbook for revenue operations teams to align marketing and sales. Highlights using data-driven content to support outbound efforts and GTM motions. Provides templates for sales ops to repurpose content effectively.

remixes.label: owned-property-imperative
remixes.hook: Every LinkedIn post you write builds LinkedIn's audience. Every blog post you write builds yours. Revenue teams keep choosing the first one and wondering why content isn't driving pipeline.
remixes.body:
Every LinkedIn post you write builds LinkedIn's audience. Every blog post you write builds yours. Revenue teams keep choosing the first one and wondering why content isn't driving pipeline.

I post on LinkedIn, X, Reddit, and a blog. But the blog is the only one that matters long-term. Everything else is distribution. A post on LinkedIn has a 48-hour shelf life. A blog post with decent SEO keeps generating traffic for months.

The distribution layer is still essential. You need the social platforms to drive attention. But attention without a destination is just noise. Every social post should route somewhere you own. A blog. A newsletter. A landing page. Something where you control the audience relationship.

I built a content pipeline that takes one piece of source content and rewrites it for four platforms. Each version is native to that platform's format and audience. Each one links back to owned property. The attribution chain is explicit: social post to site visit, site visit to email capture, email to meeting booked.

Revenue teams measuring content by impressions are optimizing the wrong metric. Impressions tell you how well the platform's algorithm treated you that day. They tell you nothing about pipeline. Traffic to owned property, email signups, demo requests from content readers. Those are the metrics that connect content to revenue.
remixes.cta: What percentage of your content efforts are building someone else's platform versus your own? Bet the split is worse than you think.

remixes.label: attribution-reality-check
remixes.hook: I asked 5 revenue leaders how they attribute pipeline to content. Three said "we don't, really." The other two described something that fell apart when I asked one follow-up question.
remixes.body:
I asked 5 revenue leaders how they attribute pipeline to content. Three said "we don't, really." The other two described something that fell apart when I asked one follow-up question.

Content attribution is broken because most teams skip the infrastructure required to make it work. They publish content, watch the impressions counter, and then present a "brand awareness" slide when the CFO asks what the content team actually produces.

Here's what working attribution looks like in my system. Every piece of content gets distributed across four platforms. Each platform version includes a tracked link to owned property. On the site, I track page views, scroll depth, and conversion events. Email signups get tagged with the content source. When a meeting gets booked, I can trace it back to which post, on which platform, drove the initial visit.

This isn't sophisticated martech. It's basic web analytics combined with disciplined link tracking and a CRM that records the source. The tools have existed for years. Teams just don't connect them because it requires the content team and the ops team to actually coordinate.

The gap between "we do content" and "we can prove content drives revenue" is entirely an infrastructure and process problem. The creative side gets all the attention. The plumbing side gets ignored. And that's why most content strategies stay in the vanity metrics loop.
remixes.cta: Can you trace a single closed deal back to a specific piece of content? If not, what's actually missing in your stack?

Here's the framework I recommend for teams serious about connecting content to revenue. It comes down to three layers.

Second, routing to owned property. Every social post routes to something you control. A blog post, a landing page, a newsletter signup. Social platforms give you reach. Owned property gives you a relationship you can nurture and measure.

Third, explicit attribution. Tracked links on every distribution point. UTM parameters. CRM source tagging. Conversion events on owned pages. When a meeting gets booked, you should be able to trace the path: which post, which platform, which page, which CTA.

Most revenue teams have the first layer partially built and nothing for layers two and three. The fix isn't more content. It's better infrastructure behind the content you already create. Start with the attribution chain and work backwards.
